Question: Provide the current commercial product datasheet and the full document list that governs the offered revision.
Why it matters: Prevents mixing pilot, concept, and current commercial data.
Evidence requested: Datasheet, document list, revision status, issue dates

Question: State whether the offered product revision is identical to the one represented on the public product page and in the pilot-validation literature. If not, list all material differences.
Why it matters: Stops false transfer of pilot or marketing claims onto a different build state.
Evidence requested: Revision-to-revision change log, superseded part list

Question: Provide the applicable installation manual, electrical manual, maintenance manual, and troubleshooting guide.
Why it matters: Closes serviceability and handover scope early.

Question: Name the technical owner for electrical design, façade integration, controls, and warranty decisions.
Why it matters: Needed for fast closure of cross-disciplinary items.
Evidence requested: Responsible person, role, email, authority boundary

Question: List all third-party components embedded in the offered configuration.
Why it matters: Needed for connector, cable, electronics, and actuator traceability.
Evidence requested: Manufacturer, part number, function, revision, country of origin

Question: Provide a section drawing showing outer lite, cavity/chamber, PV blind location, actuator location, feedthrough route, and replaceable boundaries.
Why it matters: This is the master closure item for thermal path, moisture path, service boundary, and electrical exits.
Evidence requested: Drawing PDF/DWG with dimensions, callouts, revision/date

Question: State whether the PV blind sits in the main IGU cavity or in a dedicated chamber, and quantify cavity/chamber dimensions and gas/fill assumptions.
Why it matters: Directly affects temperature, condensation risk, and optical assumptions.
Evidence requested: Section dimensions, gas or venting description, spacer concept

Question: Define the smallest independently replaceable unit for glazing, blind cassette, actuator, local electronics, and feedthrough hardware.
Why it matters: Needed to estimate downtime, spares, and façade intervention size.

Question: State whether actuation is local per window, per cassette, per mullion group, or centralized across multiple windows.
Why it matters: Changes wiring, diagnostics, controls, and common-cause failure risk.

Question: List façade integration constraints that materially affect performance or qualification (orientation, mullion depth, cavity spacing, frame material, installation tolerances).
Why it matters: Needed so yield and thermal claims are not detached from the install context.

Question: Describe drainage, condensation, and moisture-management paths relevant to the blind, feedthrough, and frame interfaces.
Why it matters: Moisture path discipline is central in enclosed moving PV assemblies.
Evidence requested: Detail drawing, seal concept, inspection points

Question: Provide the electrical datasheet for one offered window unit: Pmax, Voc, Vmp, Isc, Imp, temperature coefficients, NOCT or equivalent, maximum system voltage, and polarity conventions.
Why it matters: No architecture screening is credible without the actual unit electrical envelope.

Question: State the actual PV technology and stack used in the offered product revision, including chemistry/family, active-area fraction, and any technology-dependent constraints.
Why it matters: The public clue 'thin-film' is insufficient for qualification, temperature, and degradation analysis.
Evidence requested: Manufacturer statement, cell/module datasheet, qualification path

Question: Provide the internal electrical subdivision of one unit: number of slats, active slats, substrings/strings per slat or per zone, and the interconnection diagram.
Why it matters: This is required for mismatch modeling and fault localization.

Question: Provide the bypass topology and exact allocation: one diode per string, per substring, shared between strings, or another arrangement.
Why it matters: Bypass allocation is a first-order performance and hot-spot risk variable for this product class.

Question: Provide representative I–V and P–V curves for the actual product at multiple irradiance and temperature points, including at least one partial-shading or angle-dependent case.
Why it matters: Needed to validate MPPT compatibility and mismatch assumptions.

Question: State the allowed series and parallel aggregation limits and the design rule used to derive them.
Why it matters: Required for Voc,max/Isc,max and for preventing unsafe or nonfunctional stringing.

Question: State the required downstream power-conversion architecture: direct stringing, optimizer-based, modular AC, or other. Identify any architectures that are explicitly not allowed.
Why it matters: Avoids wasting time on architectures the product cannot support.
Evidence requested: Design manual extract, supported topologies list

Question: Define the disconnect and isolation boundary for safe maintenance, fault finding, and unit replacement.
Why it matters: This is a go/no-go serviceability and safety item.
Evidence requested: Single-line diagram, isolation procedure, lockout note

Question: State the earthing, insulation monitoring, and ground-fault or leakage-fault handling concept expected by the vendor.
Why it matters: Needed to match inverter/PCE choice and site safety design.
Evidence requested: Electrical manual, example single-line, monitoring requirements

Question: Provide frame-feedthrough details: electrical rating, dielectric path, creepage/clearance basis, strain relief, bend control, and environmental sealing.
Why it matters: Feedthroughs are one of the highest-risk hidden interfaces in the product.
Evidence requested: Feedthrough drawing, qualification/test basis, part numbers

Question: Provide the approved DC connector family, mating restrictions, and prohibited connector combinations.
Why it matters: Connector incompatibility is a recurrent PV failure source and must be controlled contractually.

Question: Provide the approved cable class, insulation type, temperature rating, voltage rating, minimum bend radius, and route limitations near moving parts or warm frame zones.
Why it matters: Needed to prevent hidden fatigue and thermal derating errors.
Evidence requested: Cable datasheet, route note, installation limits

Question: State whether reverse-current protection, string fusing, SPDs, and surge/ESD measures are required or prohibited at the unit or group level.
Why it matters: Clarifies BOS responsibilities and avoids unsafe default assumptions.

Question: Provide expected electrical signatures for common faults such as bypass failure, open substring, loss of isolation, or wrong polarity after replacement.
Why it matters: Improves commissioning scripts and remote diagnostics.
Evidence requested: Diagnostic guide, event mapping, test data

Question: Provide the controls architecture: local controller location, communication protocol(s), power supply requirements, and BMS/SCADA integration boundary.
Why it matters: Controls determine both optical behavior and operational maintainability.

Question: Provide the point list for all measured and commanded signals: slat angle, actuator status, fault state, temperature, irradiance/light, occupancy, and communication health.
Why it matters: Needed to verify observability and close the monitoring baseline.

Question: Provide actuator electrical and mechanical data: voltage, current, inrush, torque margin, duty cycle, travel time, angle resolution, and rated lifetime cycles.
Why it matters: This is required for power sizing and reliability review.

Question: State the angle-feedback method and the guaranteed error between commanded angle and actual angle over life and temperature.
Why it matters: Angle mismatch directly degrades glare control, heat gain, and energy yield.

Question: Define fail-safe behavior for loss of sensor, loss of communications, power interruption, controller reboot, and actuator stall.
Why it matters: Fail-safe state is a design requirement, not a commissioning detail.

Question: Describe manual override, local recovery, and post-fault restart behavior.
Why it matters: Needed for facility operations and troubleshooting.

Question: Provide the alarm/event dictionary with severity classes and timestamp behavior.
Why it matters: Makes remote fault localization realistic.

Question: Provide firmware-update method, rollback method, and version traceability rules.
Why it matters: Reduces configuration drift and upgrade risk in façade-integrated assets.

Question: Provide measured or modeled temperatures for slat/cell, cavity/chamber air, local electronics, and frame-exit zones under representative summer and winter conditions.
Why it matters: Thermal behavior is a first-order design unknown for glazing-integrated PV blinds.
Evidence requested: Thermal report, CFD or test report, measurement basis

Question: Provide declared operating limits, derating behavior, and protective thresholds for PV, electronics, and actuators.
Why it matters: Needed to define control limits and abnormal-condition handling.

Question: Provide the qualification basis for the offered build: IEC 61730, IEC 61215, IEC TS 63126 if applicable, and any technology-specific or in-house tests actually completed.
Why it matters: Qualification must attach to the actual offered product, not to a generic technology family.

Question: Provide evidence on humidity, condensation, seal durability, and moisture tolerance relevant to the blind chamber and electrical exits.
Why it matters: Moisture is an architecture-specific risk in enclosed moving PV assemblies.
Evidence requested: Test report, climatic test matrix, inspection criteria

Question: State whether thermography is recommended at factory acceptance, site acceptance, or in maintenance, and define the usable inspection conditions.
Why it matters: Closes the practical thermal-diagnostics pathway.

Question: Provide any known installation conditions that materially increase temperature or invalidate the standard performance basis.
Why it matters: Needed to identify high-temperature trigger conditions early.

Question: Provide building-product fire or smoke related declarations that the façade team must account for, if applicable to the offered assembly.
Why it matters: Prevents a late handoff gap between PV and façade/fire reviewers.

Question: Provide actuator cycle-life evidence under the actual load/environment envelope of the offered product.
Why it matters: Moving-element reliability is central and cannot be inferred from generic blind hardware.

Question: Provide evidence for cable/harness flex life or motion-fatigue control at all moving electrical interfaces.
Why it matters: Intermittent motion-related conductor failures are difficult to localize after installation.
Evidence requested: Motion-cycle test, bend-life data, design note

Question: Provide feedthrough and seal-interface reliability evidence under temperature cycling, humidity, and service handling.
Why it matters: This is one of the highest-risk latent-fault interfaces in the system.
Evidence requested: Test report, design verification plan, warranty note

Question: Provide field failure modes seen to date and the current corrective-action status for each significant mode.
Why it matters: Field feedback is often more informative than generic MTBF language.

Question: Provide expected replacement times and special access needs for L1/L2/L3/L4 interventions.
Why it matters: Needed for downtime and access-cost modeling.
Evidence requested: Service procedure, required tools, labor assumptions

Question: Provide the actual replacement procedure for a failed unit, including electrical isolation steps, recommissioning checks, and risk of misconnection.
Why it matters: Service procedure quality is a major differentiator for enclosed façade hardware.

Question: Provide preventive-maintenance intervals, inspection points, consumables, and explicit no-maintenance claims or exclusions.
Why it matters: Needed to turn 'low maintenance' into an auditable O&M statement.

Question: Provide the spare-parts strategy and parts availability horizon for actuators, controllers, connectors, and special glass/blind subassemblies.
Why it matters: Long lead items drive lifecycle risk in façade retrofits.
Evidence requested: Part list, lead times, obsolescence policy

Question: Provide the standard commissioning package: single-line, labeling scheme, point list, acceptance tests, and record sheets.
Why it matters: This closes the IEC 62446-style handover gap before procurement.

Question: State the required pre-energization, energization, and functional tests for first power-up and post-replacement recommissioning.
Why it matters: Needed for safe startup and clean service loops.

Question: Provide acceptance limits or tolerances for key values: measured Voc, current, angle accuracy, telemetry availability, and temperature baseline.
Why it matters: Without pass/fail limits, a commissioning checklist is only paperwork.

Question: Provide the required as-built labels and naming conventions for units, groups, feedthroughs, and control points.
Why it matters: Makes later diagnostics and part replacement tractable.

Question: Provide a sample event log or SCADA export showing normal operation, alarm behavior, and fault reset behavior.
Why it matters: Reduces ambiguity in monitoring/BMS integration.

Question: State training, witnessing, or vendor attendance requirements for FAT, SAT, first energization, and warranty-preserving maintenance.
Why it matters: These conditions can materially affect deployment cost and schedule.

Question: Provide warranty terms by subsystem: glass, PV performance, blind mechanics, actuators, electronics, controls software, and connectors/feedthroughs.
Why it matters: Subsystem-split warranty terms matter more than a single headline number.

Question: List warranty exclusions triggered by non-approved power electronics, control logic, mounting details, cleaning, or service practices.
Why it matters: Needed to keep architecture and O&M decisions contract-safe.

Question: Provide indicative lead times, minimum order conditions, and any long-lead custom parts relevant to pilot and scale-up deployment.
Why it matters: Directly affects roadmap realism and retrofit planning.

Question: Provide reference projects or pilot installations relevant to similar façade orientation, climate, and operational profile.
Why it matters: Useful for benchmarking and identifying hidden deployment constraints.
Evidence requested: Project list, contactability limits, installed dates
